preamble: The phonetic transformation from an alveolar trill [r] to a voiceless alveolar plosive [t] represents a process of fortition, where a sound's articulation becomes 'stronger' or more constricted. This shift involves the cessation of the rapid, intermittent contact characteristic of a trill, replaced by a complete closure and release typical of a plosive. It also entails a change in voicing if the original trill was voiced, resulting in a voiceless stop.
phoneticEffects: Fortition, de-trilling, plosivization, devoicing (if the source trill is voiced), assimilation (context-dependent)

examples.note: Fortition of the alveolar trill to an aspirated voiceless alveolar plosive in post-nasal contexts.
Assimilation of a rhotic /r/ to a following alveolar stop /t/, as seen in verb conjugations (e.g., kar-ta > katta 'shear (past)').

sources: Hyman, L. J., Mathangwane, J. T., & Mooko, B. T. (2016). Setswana (South African). Journal of the International Phonetic Association, 46(1), 101-118.
Odden, D. (2005). Introducing Phonology. Cambridge University Press.
